title: Multiple fidelities and joint numerical range
abstract: We investigate the effectiveness of entanglement detection based on multiple fidelities via the geometry of the joint separable numerical range. When all reference states are product states, we derive a necessary and sufficient criterion for such detection: either some pair of reference states has nontrivial moduli of the local inner products on both subsystems, or the orthogonal complement of the span of the reference states is completely entangled. We further show that there exist sets of reference product states for which no proper subset is effective for entanglement detection, whereas the full set is. A typical example of this phenomenon is provided by unextendible product bases. Moreover, for a pair of reference product states on a bipartite system with arbitrary local dimensions, we characterize both the joint numerical range and the joint separable numerical range, showing that the joint separable numerical range is determined solely by their local fidelities, as illustrated by a representative two-qubit example. Our results offer a systematic approach to designing effective entanglement witnesses and lay the groundwork for extensions to higher-dimensional and multipartite scenarios.
summary: The paper establishes a rigorous geometric framework to determine when a set of product-state fidelities can successfully detect entanglement. It identifies that detection power depends on the local overlap of reference states or the existence of completely entangled subspaces. This provides a blueprint for constructing effective entanglement witnesses in arbitrary dimensions.
main_problem: Determining the conditions under which a set of reference product states can effectively detect entanglement using multiple fidelities.
main_result: The authors derive a necessary and sufficient criterion for effective entanglement detection: either a pair of reference states must have nontrivial local inner product moduli on both subsystems, or the orthogonal complement of the span of the reference states must be a completely entangled subspace.
method: A geometric approach utilizing the Joint Numerical Range (JNR) and the Joint Separable Numerical Range (JSNR) combined with algebraic geometry and subspace analysis.
model_or_system: Bipartite quantum systems with arbitrary local dimensions (dA x dB) using reference pure product states to certify the entanglement of a target state.
key_observables: Multiple fidelities (expectation values of product-state projectors) and local fidelities (moduli of local inner products).
important_parameters: Local inner product moduli (cA and cB), global fidelity (c), and the structure of the orthogonal complement of the reference state span.
main_assumptions: The reference states are assumed to be linearly independent product states; the analysis of the JSNR boundary is specifically addressed for product-state projectors.
figures_summary: A comparison between the JNR and JSNR for two reference product states, illustrating the JNR boundary as a conic section and the JSNR as a symmetric, piecewise-segment boundary.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multiple fidelity entanglement detection, develops a geometric framework using JNR and JSNR, provides necessary and sufficient criteria for k=2 and k>2 cases, demonstrates collective effectiveness using unextendible product bases, and characterizes the JSNR geometry for two product states.
why_interesting: This provides a systematic, geometric method for designing optimal entanglement witnesses, which is highly relevant for experimental verification of entanglement in bipartite and multipartite quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
